facts.text: Ensley received its name in 1925, honoring Fred B., a local figure whose legacy lives in the town’s identity.
During the mid‑20th century, Ensley became known as the original site of the T., a landmark that shaped the area’s early economy.
The community first grew as a rural agricultural hub in the late 1800s, with farmers cultivating fig trees, Detroit plums, and Satsuma oranges.
After World War II, Ensley expanded rapidly, fueled by its proximity to Pensacola Naval Air Station and the surrounding military‑industry boom.
Ensley sits on a key transportation corridor, with US 90 Alt, US 29, and I‑10 all passing through the town’s center.
Because it lies on the Florida Panhandle, Ensley is especially vulnerable to hurricanes, a fact that has shaped its emergency planning.
